002 — Baseline classifiers (mlr3) on the labelled SNI feature matrix

Author

Dan MacLean

Published

April 23, 2026

Goal

Quantify how well the canonical SNI features distinguish helper from sensor proteins on the 18-protein labelled benchmark, using an mlr3 pipeline under leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV). Three feature subsets — Scientist, Co-Scientist, and Combined — are evaluated under two strategies for the α1-NA features (drop them, or median-impute them). Observed LOOCV accuracies and AUCs are then compared against permutation null distributions obtained by re-running the same pipeline on label-shuffled data.

The idea here is that because we’re setting up an mlr3 factory-style classifier pipeline, we can be maximalist about what sub-sets/super-sets to look at. We can therefore compare the Co and Scientist features at low effort and the effect of the missing value features.

We are optimistic for good classifiers because of the prevalance of single features that do excellent sensor/helper classification on there own.

Hypotheses

  1. Multiple single features already give perfect rank separation on this benchmark (notebook 001), so multivariate LOOCV accuracy will be at or near 1.0 for at least the Scientist and Combined subsets under either α1-policy.
  2. The Scientist and Co-Scientist subsets, evaluated on the same 18 proteins, will give LOOCV scores that are not distinguishable from each other, since one misclassification at n = 18 corresponds to a change of 1/18 ≈ 0.056 in accuracy.
  3. The permutation null distribution of LOOCV accuracy will be roughly centred on 0.5; empirical p-values for the observed scores will be bounded by 1 / (n_perms + 1) when no shuffled labelling matches.

Rationale

n = 18 is small. We use (a) LOOCV so every sample serves once as test, (b) more than one learner so the result is not a property of a single model class, (c) explicit α1-feature handling — drop vs median-impute — to separate the contribution of the structurally missing features from that of the always-observed features, and (d) a permutation null so observed scores can be read against the distribution produced by the same pipeline on randomly shuffled labels.

Setup

Code 
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(mlr3)
  library(mlr3learners)
  library(mlr3pipelines)
  library(here)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(tidyr)
  library(data.table)
})
lgr::get_logger("mlr3")$set_threshold("warn")

knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.path = "../results/figures/002_")

mat_11 <- readRDS(here("data", "feature_matrix_labelled_11.rds"))
mat_11$class <- factor(mat_11$class, levels = c("sensor", "helper"))

dir.create(here("results"), showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

1. Feature subsets and design cells

The “Scientist”, “Co-Scientist” and “Both” subsets are taken from SolNRCH_foldome/R/NRC_heatmap_analysis.R. The α1-NA set is the three columns shown in notebook 001 to be NA in 9/9 sensors.

Code 
sci_full   <- c("ipTM_LCB", "Sum_CONTACTS", "S_PROTO", "L_APEX_mean")
cos_full   <- c("D_MHD_P_mean", "THETA_APEX_mean", "H_ABS_mean", "MU_H_mean")
both       <- c("BSA_INT_PROTO", "SD_THETA_ROT", "D_APEX")
combined   <- unique(c(sci_full, cos_full, both))     # 11 features

alpha1_na  <- c("THETA_APEX_mean", "L_APEX_mean", "MU_H_mean")

make_cell <- function(name, features, impute) {
  list(name = name, features = features, impute = impute)
}

design <- list(
  make_cell("scientist_drop_a1",     setdiff(sci_full, alpha1_na),   FALSE),
  make_cell("scientist_impute_a1",   sci_full,                       TRUE),
  make_cell("coscientist_drop_a1",   setdiff(cos_full, alpha1_na),   FALSE),
  make_cell("coscientist_impute_a1", cos_full,                       TRUE),
  make_cell("combined_drop_a1",      setdiff(combined, alpha1_na),   FALSE),
  make_cell("combined_impute_a1",    combined,                       TRUE)
)

design_summary <- do.call(rbind, lapply(design, function(d) {
  data.frame(
    cell        = d$name,
    impute_a1   = d$impute,
    n_features  = length(d$features),
    features    = paste(d$features, collapse = ", ")
  )
}))
knitr::kable(design_summary)
cell impute_a1 n_features features
scientist_drop_a1 FALSE 3 ipTM_LCB, Sum_CONTACTS, S_PROTO
scientist_impute_a1 TRUE 4 ipTM_LCB, Sum_CONTACTS, S_PROTO, L_APEX_mean
coscientist_drop_a1 FALSE 2 D_MHD_P_mean, H_ABS_mean
coscientist_impute_a1 TRUE 4 D_MHD_P_mean, THETA_APEX_mean, H_ABS_mean, MU_H_mean
combined_drop_a1 FALSE 8 ipTM_LCB, Sum_CONTACTS, S_PROTO, D_MHD_P_mean, H_ABS_mean, BSA_INT_PROTO, SD_THETA_ROT, D_APEX
combined_impute_a1 TRUE 11 ipTM_LCB, Sum_CONTACTS, S_PROTO, L_APEX_mean, D_MHD_P_mean, THETA_APEX_mean, H_ABS_mean, MU_H_mean, BSA_INT_PROTO, SD_THETA_ROT, D_APEX

2. Pipeline helpers

A small set of helpers wraps the mlr3 task / learner / resample boilerplate so each design cell × learner combination is one function call.

Code 
make_task <- function(data, features, target = "class") {
  d <- as.data.frame(data[, c(target, features), drop = FALSE])
  TaskClassif$new(id = "sni", backend = d, target = target,
                  positive = "helper")
}

build_learner <- function(lrn_id, impute) {
  base <- lrn(lrn_id, predict_type = "prob")
  if (lrn_id == "classif.ranger") base$param_set$values$num.trees <- 200
  if (impute) {
    g <- po("imputemedian",
            affect_columns = selector_type(c("numeric", "integer"))) %>>% base
    GraphLearner$new(g)
  } else {
    base
  }
}

run_loocv <- function(data, features, lrn_id, impute) {
  tsk <- make_task(data, features)
  lrn_obj <- build_learner(lrn_id, impute)
  rr <- suppressWarnings(resample(tsk, lrn_obj, rsmp("loo")))
  # AUC is undefined per LOO fold (one observation each); score on the
  # pooled prediction across folds so the 9-helper / 9-sensor ranking is
  # used as a single sample.
  acc_mean <- rr$aggregate(msr("classif.acc"))
  auc_pool <- rr$prediction()$score(msr("classif.auc"))
  list(rr = rr,
       agg = c(classif.acc = unname(acc_mean),
               classif.auc = unname(auc_pool)))
}

3. Baseline benchmark

Three learners types — featureless (predicts the majority class), log_reg (plain logistic regression), and ranger (random forest, 200 trees) — are run on each of the six design cells. featureless gives the no-information baseline by bascically calling just the class with the most members as its prediction for everything log_reg and ranger give two contrasting model classes (linear and non-linear ensemble).

Code 
LEARNERS <- c("classif.featureless", "classif.log_reg", "classif.ranger")

set.seed(1)
bench_results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(design, function(cell) {
  do.call(rbind, lapply(LEARNERS, function(lrn_id) {
    res <- tryCatch(
      run_loocv(mat_11, cell$features, lrn_id, cell$impute),
      error = function(e) list(agg = c(classif.acc = NA_real_,
                                       classif.auc = NA_real_))
    )
    data.frame(
      cell    = cell$name,
      impute  = cell$impute,
      learner = sub("^classif\\.", "", lrn_id),
      acc     = unname(res$agg["classif.acc"]),
      auc     = unname(res$agg["classif.auc"])
    )
  }))
}))

bench_results

Ok, so our benchmark is helpful. featureless scores 0 — not a chance result but an LOOCV artefact: with balanced classes, holding out a helper flips the training majority to sensor (and vice versa), so featureless is wrong every fold. Read it as “no feature information used” rather than a literal 0% accuracy. Past that, the headline is combined + ranger: both the drop and impute cells hit perfect LOOCV accuracy and AUC, so the full 8–11 feature set is where the real classifier lives. The Scientist-only and Co-Scientist-only subsets are both strong but trail combined by a misclassification or two. The models discern sensors and helpers pretty well.

Code 
wide_acc <- pivot_wider(bench_results[, c("cell", "learner", "acc")],
                        names_from = learner, values_from = acc)
wide_auc <- pivot_wider(bench_results[, c("cell", "learner", "auc")],
                        names_from = learner, values_from = auc)
knitr::kable(wide_acc, digits = 3, caption = "LOOCV accuracy")
LOOCV accuracy
cell featureless log_reg ranger
scientist_drop_a1 0 0.944 0.944
scientist_impute_a1 0 0.944 0.944
coscientist_drop_a1 0 0.889 0.889
coscientist_impute_a1 0 0.833 0.833
combined_drop_a1 0 0.889 1.000
combined_impute_a1 0 0.889 1.000

knitr::kable(wide_auc, digits = 3, caption = "LOOCV AUC")
LOOCV AUC
cell featureless log_reg ranger
scientist_drop_a1 0 1.000 1.000
scientist_impute_a1 0 1.000 1.000
coscientist_drop_a1 0 0.778 0.889
coscientist_impute_a1 0 0.753 0.926
combined_drop_a1 0 0.975 1.000

Same thing in a wider table.

4. Held-out predictions per protein

For the two “combined” cells (drop α1 vs impute α1), report the LOOCV-held-out predicted probability of helper for each protein, under the random-forest learner. This shows which proteins, if any, are misclassified or sit close to the 0.5 boundary. Basically, we’ll leave out each protein in turn, retrain and see what its \(p(helper)\) is.

Code 
held_out <- function(data, features, lrn_id, impute) {
  tsk <- make_task(data, features)
  lrn_obj <- build_learner(lrn_id, impute)
  rr <- suppressWarnings(resample(tsk, lrn_obj, rsmp("loo")))
  pred_dt <- as.data.table(rr$prediction())
  pred_dt$ID    <- data$ID[pred_dt$row_ids]
  pred_dt$class <- data$class[pred_dt$row_ids]
  pred_dt[, .(ID, class, response, prob_helper = prob.helper)]
}

ho_drop   <- held_out(mat_11, setdiff(combined, alpha1_na),
                      "classif.ranger", impute = FALSE)
ho_impute <- held_out(mat_11, combined, "classif.ranger", impute = TRUE)

ho_drop$cell   <- "combined_drop_a1"
ho_impute$cell <- "combined_impute_a1"
ho_all <- rbind(ho_drop, ho_impute)
ho_all <- ho_all[order(ho_all$cell, ho_all$class, -ho_all$prob_helper)]
knitr::kable(ho_all, digits = 3)
ID class response prob_helper cell

Code 
ggplot(ho_all, aes(x = prob_helper, y = reorder(ID, prob_helper),
                   colour = class, shape = response == class)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ cell) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(sensor = "#e76f51", helper = "#264653")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = 16, `FALSE` = 4),
                     labels = c(`TRUE` = "correct", `FALSE` = "wrong"),
                     name = "prediction") +
  labs(x = "LOOCV held-out P(helper) (ranger)", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

They’re all re-predicted correctly and most \(p(helper)\) stays appreciably away from the 0.5 zone of not being so sure.

5. Permutation null distribution

Class labels are permuted across the 18 proteins and the LOOCV pipeline is re-run on each shuffle. n_perms = 200 shuffles per cell, ranger learner only (chosen for speed; the same approach applies to the other learners). Empirical one-sided p-values are (1 + sum(null >= obs)) / (n_perms + 1).

Standard ‘bootstrap-like’ shuffling of labels for 200 reps. The idea being that we should see a nice aggregation of LOOCV accuracy (and AUC) around 0.5. Which would indicate that the random shuffling makes the model unable to recall the original labels. Randomly labelled models should not be classifiable, only those with real patterns in data should.

The result is cached to results/permutation_results.rds; delete that file to force recomputation.

Code 
N_PERMS <- 200
PERM_CACHE <- here("results", "permutation_results.rds")

run_permutations <- function(data, features, lrn_id, impute,
                             n_perms, seed) {
  set.seed(seed)
  out <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = n_perms, ncol = 2,
                dimnames = list(NULL, c("acc", "auc")))
  for (i in seq_len(n_perms)) {
    d_perm <- data
    d_perm$class <- sample(d_perm$class)
    res <- tryCatch(
      run_loocv(d_perm, features, lrn_id, impute),
      error = function(e) list(agg = c(classif.acc = NA_real_,
                                       classif.auc = NA_real_))
    )
    out[i, ] <- c(res$agg["classif.acc"], res$agg["classif.auc"])
  }
  as.data.frame(out)
}

if (file.exists(PERM_CACHE)) {
  perm_results <- readRDS(PERM_CACHE)
  message("Loaded cached permutation results from ", PERM_CACHE)
} else {
  message("Running ", N_PERMS, " permutations per cell (ranger)...")
  t0 <- Sys.time()
  perm_results <- lapply(seq_along(design), function(i) {
    cell <- design[[i]]
    obs <- run_loocv(mat_11, cell$features, "classif.ranger", cell$impute)$agg
    nulls <- run_permutations(mat_11, cell$features, "classif.ranger",
                              cell$impute, N_PERMS, seed = 100 + i)
    list(cell = cell$name, obs = obs, nulls = nulls)
  })
  names(perm_results) <- vapply(design, `[[`, character(1), "name")
  saveRDS(perm_results, PERM_CACHE)
  message("Done in ", round(as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))),
          " s")
}
Code 
perm_summary <- do.call(rbind, lapply(perm_results, function(r) {
  data.frame(
    cell      = r$cell,
    obs_acc   = unname(r$obs["classif.acc"]),
    obs_auc   = unname(r$obs["classif.auc"]),
    null_acc_mean = mean(r$nulls$acc, na.rm = TRUE),
    null_auc_mean = mean(r$nulls$auc, na.rm = TRUE),
    p_acc     = (1 + sum(r$nulls$acc >= r$obs["classif.acc"], na.rm = TRUE)) /
                (sum(!is.na(r$nulls$acc)) + 1),
    p_auc     = (1 + sum(r$nulls$auc >= r$obs["classif.auc"], na.rm = TRUE)) /
                (sum(!is.na(r$nulls$auc)) + 1)
  )
}))
knitr::kable(perm_summary, digits = 3, row.names = FALSE)
cell obs_acc obs_auc null_acc_mean null_auc_mean p_acc p_auc
scientist_drop_a1 0.944 1.000 0.434 0.356 0.005 0.005
scientist_impute_a1 0.944 1.000 0.443 0.374 0.005 0.005
coscientist_drop_a1 0.889 0.877 0.446 0.399 0.005 0.010
coscientist_impute_a1 0.833 0.951 0.454 0.408 0.015 0.005
combined_drop_a1 1.000 1.000 0.436 0.356 0.005 0.005
combined_impute_a1 1.000 1.000 0.432 0.364 0.005 0.005
Code 
nulls_long <- do.call(rbind, lapply(perm_results, function(r) {
  data.frame(cell = r$cell,
             metric = rep(c("acc", "auc"), each = nrow(r$nulls)),
             value  = c(r$nulls$acc, r$nulls$auc))
}))
obs_long <- do.call(rbind, lapply(perm_results, function(r) {
  data.frame(cell = r$cell,
             metric = c("acc", "auc"),
             value  = unname(c(r$obs["classif.acc"], r$obs["classif.auc"])))
}))

ggplot(nulls_long, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "grey70", colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(data = obs_long, aes(xintercept = value),
             colour = "#ED2939", linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_grid(cell ~ metric, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(x = "Score under permuted labels (red = observed)", y = "Permutations") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

Indeed thats what we see. Most shuffled-label runs sit around 0.5 on both acc and AUC. The red line is our observed score (not a significance cut-off) — the p-value is just the fraction of shuffles that reach or exceed it. For most cells no shuffle matches, so p hits the 1/(200+1) ≈ 0.005 floor; where a handful do, p just inches up. Observed scores this far above the shuffle pile indicate a real pattern.

6. Conclusion

The combined data and ranger models give a great classification of sensors from helpers using the SNI features.
